summary: Italian-American film director from New York City, born 1942. Best known for Taxi Driver, Goodfellas, Raging Bull, and Mean Streets. Longtime collaborator with Robert De Niro, editor Thelma Schoonmaker, and screenwriter Paul Schrader. Also a major film preservationist through his World Cinema Project. Known for rock music soundtracks, tracking shots, and slow-motion cinematography.

works.name: Taxi Driver (1976)
works.description: Scorsese's breakthrough film about disturbed Vietnam vet Travis Bickle (Robert De Niro), who becomes obsessed with saving teenage prostitute Iris (Jodie Foster, age 12). Famous for the mirror scene ('You talkin' to me?'), scripted by Paul Schrader. Scorsese himself cameos as a man who says he'll kill his wife with a .44 magnum. Camera work influenced by Hitchcock's The Wrong Man. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Travis Bickle, a disturbed Vietnam vet, becomes obsessed with saving underage prostitute Iris (Jodie Foster), eventually planning to assassinate senator Charles Palatine

works.name: Goodfellas (1990)
works.description: Crime film about real-life mobster Henry Hill (Ray Liotta), who opens with 'As far back as I can remember, I always wanted to be a gangster.' Joe Pesci plays Tommy DeVito, murdered after being tricked into thinking he's a 'made man.' One character is called Jimmy Two-Times for repeating himself. Famous for the Copacabana long-take tracking shot following Henry Hill and his girlfriend. Edited by Thelma Schoonmaker. Wikipedia

works.name: Raging Bull (1980)
works.description: Black-and-white biographical film about boxer Jake LaMotta, played by Robert De Niro. Opening credits accompanied by Mascagni's 'Cavalleria Rusticana: Intermezzo.' LaMotta looks into a mirror reciting Brando's 'I coulda been a contender' from On the Waterfront. Edited by Thelma Schoonmaker, who received an Oscar nomination for this film. Scorsese's self-identified 'best film' (the documentary Italianamerican actually holds that title for him). Wikipedia

works.name: Mean Streets (1973)
works.description: Scorsese's breakthrough film (his first collaboration with De Niro). Set among low-level New York Italian-American mobsters. Protagonist Charlie reckons with his epileptic girlfriend Teresa and covers for his debt-ridden friend Johnny Boy. Harvey Keitel also features. Opening features credits over home movies. Cream's 'Steppin' Out' plays as Teresa's car crashes into a fire hydrant at the end. John Cassavetes told Scorsese his previous film (Boxcar Bertha) was 'a piece of shit,' prompting Mean Streets. Compared to Wong Kar-Wai's As Tears Go By. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Scorsese's first collaboration with Robert De Niro and Harvey Keitel; protagonist Charlie covers for debt-ridden Johnny Boy and has an affair with his epileptic cousin Teresa
examples: The protagonist of one of his films has a secret affair with his epileptic cousin and holds his hand over open flames in a restaurant kitchen and in church.

examples: At the end of this director's breakthrough film, Cream's 'Steppin' Out' plays on the radio before a car containing Amy Robinson's Teresa crashes into a fire hydrant.

clues.clue: Made after John Cassavetes told Scorsese his previous film Boxcar Bertha was 'a piece of shit'; was itself inspired by Fellini's I Vitelloni

works.name: The Departed (2006)
works.description: Crime film adapted from Hong Kong's Infernal Affairs. Set in Boston (replacing Hong Kong's setting). Stars Jack Nicholson and Matt Damon. Scorsese's sole Best Director Oscar win (2007 Academy Awards). Features Scorsese's characteristic use of circular iris shots. Wikipedia

works.name: The Irishman (2019)
works.description: Historical drama about Frank Sheeran, based on the unverified book I Heard You Paint Houses by Charles Brandt. About Sheeran's interactions with the Philadelphia mob and the Teamsters. Used CGI to 'de-age' main actors without motion tracking markers. Al Pacino's first collaboration with Scorsese; Pacino plays a character who refuses to drink and hates when people are late. Partially inspired by Once Upon a Time in America (which Scorsese also restored). Wikipedia

works.name: The Last Waltz (1978)
works.description: Concert film about The Band's final concert. Sometimes considered the greatest concert film ever made. Rotoscoping was used. Features interludes with poets including Michael McClure and Lawrence Ferlinghetti. A 'traveling booger matte' was invented to obscure cocaine in Neil Young's nose. Wikipedia

works.name: Hugo (2011)
works.description: Film about a young French boy (Asa Butterfield) whose dead father tried to repair an automaton. The boy befriends Isabelle, daughter of Georges Méliès. Inspired by Brian Selznick's novel The Invention of Hugo Cabret. Wikipedia

works.name: Killers of the Flower Moon (2023)
works.description: 2023 epic about the Osage murders in Oklahoma. Lily Gladstone translated scenes into Osage and helped restructure the film to avoid a 'white-savior' narrative. Based on David Grann's book. Wikipedia

works.name: Italianamerican (1974) / Documentaries
works.description: Scorsese has called Italianamerican — a documentary about his parents Catherine and Charles at their Elizabeth Street apartment — his own personal 'best film.' The credits include a recipe for meatballs. He also made The Last Waltz, A Personal Journey through American Movies (1995, BBC; classifies directors as storytellers, illusionists, smugglers, iconoclasts), Public Speaking, My Voyage in Italy (4 hours, about Italian cinema), No Direction Home (Bob Dylan), George Harrison: Living in the Material World, and Shine a Light.

works.name: World Cinema Project
works.description: Scorsese's nonprofit dedicated to film preservation and restoration. Has restored films including Ousmane Sembene's Black Girl, Lino Brocka's Manila in the Claws of Light, The Chess Game of the Wind (Iranian), Once Upon a Time in America (Leone), The Cloud-Capped Star (Bengali), A Brighter Summer Day, and Mysterious Object at Noon.

clues.clue: Founded the World Cinema Project for film preservation; restored Ousmane Sembene's Black Girl, Lino Brocka's Manila in the Claws of Light, Iranian Chess Game of the Wind, Leone's Once Upon a Time in America, and many others

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Martin Scorsese is an Italian-American director from New York City's Little Italy (Elizabeth Street). His frequent collaborators include screenwriter Paul Schrader (Taxi Driver, Raging Bull), editor Thelma Schoonmaker (tied for most Oscar nominations in her category), and actor Robert De Niro. Scorsese had a cameo as Van Gogh in Kurosawa's Dreams. He directed the pilot of Boardwalk Empire. He denounced Marvel franchise films as lacking in art. Saul Bass created title sequences for his films. Rodrigo Prieto serves as a recent regular cinematographer. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Paul Schrader wrote screenplays for Taxi Driver and Raging Bull; Schrader's The Transcendental Style in Film analyzes Dreyer, Bresson, and Ozu
examples: Schrader is better known for his collaborations with this director of Taxi Driver and Raging Bull.

comprehensive_summary:
Martin Scorsese is an Italian-American film director born in 1942 in New York City's Little Italy neighborhood. His most frequently clued film is Taxi Driver (1976), scripted by Paul Schrader, in which Robert De Niro plays Travis Bickle, a disturbed Vietnam vet who becomes obsessed with rescuing underage prostitute Iris (played by 12-year-old Jodie Foster) and plans to assassinate Senator Charles Palatine. The famous 'You talkin' to me?' mirror monologue was improvised by De Niro. Scorsese himself cameos as a man planning to kill his wife with a .44 magnum. Travis gives himself a Mohawk when planning to kill the senator — John Hinckley emulated this before shooting Ronald Reagan. Jodie Foster wore red wedge shoes as Iris. Scorsese used a 'priest's-eye view' shot for the brothel massacre aftermath. Camera work was influenced by Hitchcock's The Wrong Man. The film's title comes from Raymond Chandler's phrase about a hero going down 'mean streets.'

Goodfellas (1990) stars Ray Liotta as Henry Hill, who opens with 'As far back as I can remember, I always wanted to be a gangster.' Joe Pesci plays Tommy DeVito, murdered after being tricked into thinking he's a made man. One character is called 'Jimmy Two-Times' for repeating himself. The film is famous for its Copacabana long-take tracking shot following Henry Hill and his girlfriend. Raging Bull (1980) is a black-and-white film about boxer Jake LaMotta (De Niro), opening with slow-motion boxing footage set to Mascagni's 'Cavalleria Rusticana: Intermezzo.' LaMotta ends the film quoting Brando's 'I coulda been a contender' monologue from On the Waterfront (a Kazan film). Mean Streets (1973) was Scorsese's first collaboration with De Niro and Harvey Keitel; Charlie covers for debt-ridden Johnny Boy and has an affair with his epileptic cousin Teresa. It ends with Cream's 'Steppin' Out' playing as Teresa's car hits a fire hydrant. Cassavetes told Scorsese his previous Boxcar Bertha was 'a piece of shit,' spurring Mean Streets; the film was itself inspired by Fellini's I Vitelloni.

The Departed (2006) adapted Hong Kong's Infernal Affairs to a Boston setting, starring Jack Nicholson and Matt Damon — it gave Scorsese his sole Best Director Academy Award. The Irishman (2019) based on I Heard You Paint Houses (Charles Brandt) follows Frank Sheeran among the Philadelphia mob and Teamsters; de-aging CGI was used without motion tracking markers; Al Pacino's first Scorsese collaboration. Hugo (2011) stars Asa Butterfield as a French orphan whose father repaired an automaton; the boy befriends Isabelle, daughter of Méliès. Killers of the Flower Moon (2023) depicts the Osage murders; Lily Gladstone translated dialogue and helped restructure the film away from a white-savior narrative.

Scorsese's documentary Italianamerican — about his parents Catherine and Charles at their Elizabeth Street apartment — is his own self-identified 'best film'; the credits include a meatball recipe. The Last Waltz (1978) is his concert film of The Band's final show, often called the greatest concert film ever made; a 'traveling booger matte' was invented to obscure cocaine in Neil Young's nose. His 1995 BBC documentary A Personal Journey through American Movies classifies directors as storytellers, illusionists, smugglers, and iconoclasts. Other documentaries cover Bob Dylan (No Direction Home), the Rolling Stones (Shine a Light), and George Harrison (Living in the Material World, 3.5 hours).

Scorsese founded the World Cinema Project to restore non-Western films, including Ousmane Sembene's Black Girl, Lino Brocka's Manila in the Claws of Light, Iran's Chess Game of the Wind, Leone's Once Upon a Time in America, Bengali film The Cloud-Capped Star, and others. Key collaborators: screenwriter Paul Schrader (Taxi Driver, Raging Bull), editor Thelma Schoonmaker (tied with Michael Kahn for most Oscar editing nominations), Robert De Niro, and (later) Leonardo DiCaprio. Scorsese had a cameo as Van Gogh in Kurosawa's Dreams. He directed the pilot of Boardwalk Empire. He famously said Marvel films lack artistic value. Saul Bass designed title sequences for his films, and he uses circular iris shots as a trademark device.
